name: Algeria
facts: Largest country in Africa by area
Sahara Desert covers over 80% of the territory
Home to Timgad — the 'Pompeii of Africa'
Algeria has the largest military in Africa
Tuareg people navigate the Sahara by century-old star maps

name: Angola
facts: Portuguese is the official language — Angola was a Portuguese colony for nearly 500 years until 1975
Home to the Welwitschia plant, which can live over 1,500 years and has just two leaves
The Kalandula Falls are among the largest waterfalls in Africa by volume
Has the world's largest known population of giant sable antelope, found nowhere else
Capital Luanda was once the most expensive city in the world for expatriates

name: Benin
facts: Benin is the birthplace of the Vodun (Voodoo) religion, still practiced by ~40% of the population
Ganvié is a stilt village of around 20,000 people built entirely on Lake Nokoué
The Kingdom of Dahomey had an all-female military regiment — the 'Dahomey Amazons'
Cotonou is the de facto economic capital, while Porto-Novo is the official capital
Benin was a major hub of the trans-Atlantic slave trade — the 'Door of No Return' stands in Ouidah

name: Botswana
facts: The Okavango Delta is one of the world's largest inland river deltas — a UNESCO site
Botswana has the highest density of African elephants on Earth — over 130,000
Diamonds make up roughly 80% of Botswana's exports — discovered just after independence in 1966
Around 70% of the country is covered by the Kalahari Desert
The pula (currency) means 'rain' in Setswana — a sign of value in this dry land

name: Burkina Faso
facts: The name means 'Land of Honest People' — chosen by revolutionary leader Thomas Sankara in 1984
Hosts FESPACO, Africa's largest film festival, every two years in Ouagadougou
Mossi people make up about half the population, with empires dating back to the 11th century
One of the world's top producers of cotton — known as 'white gold'
The Lobi people build fortress-like compounds called 'soukala' from earth and wood

name: Burundi
facts: One of the smallest countries in Africa but among the most densely populated
Drumming is a sacred art — the Royal Drummers of Burundi are UNESCO-recognized
Lake Tanganyika, the world's second-deepest lake, forms its western border
The source of the Nile's longest tributary, the Ruvyironza, lies in southern Burundi
Coffee and tea make up the vast majority of Burundi's export earnings

name: Cameroon
facts: Called 'Africa in miniature' for its mix of geography — beaches, deserts, mountains, rainforest, savanna
Mount Cameroon is one of the largest active volcanoes in Africa, last erupting in 2012
Both English and French are official languages — a legacy of British and French rule
Lake Nyos infamously released a CO₂ cloud in 1986 that killed over 1,700 people
The Indomitable Lions are one of Africa's most successful football teams

name: Cape Verde
facts: An archipelago of 10 volcanic islands off the coast of West Africa — uninhabited until Portuguese arrived in 1456
Cesária Évora, the 'Barefoot Diva', made the genre of morna world-famous
Pico do Fogo is an active volcano whose last major eruption was in 2014–2015
The official name was changed from Cape Verde to Cabo Verde in 2013 — even in English
Most Cape Verdeans live abroad — the diaspora is larger than the resident population

name: Central African Republic
facts: Sangha Trinational rainforest is a UNESCO site shared with Cameroon and the Republic of the Congo
Boganda, the founding leader, designed the flag's distinctive cross of blue/white/green/yellow + red
Holds significant deposits of diamonds, gold, and uranium
Forest elephants, lowland gorillas and bongos roam the Dzanga-Sangha reserve
The Aka 'pygmy' people are renowned for polyphonic forest music recognized by UNESCO

name: Chad
alphabetExample: Examples: A, B, C — ا, ب, ت
facts: Lake Chad once covered over 25,000 km² but has shrunk by ~90% since the 1960s
The Sahara Desert covers the entire northern half of the country
Home to Zakouma National Park, where elephant numbers have rebounded after near-extinction
Toumaï, a 7-million-year-old hominid skull found in Chad, is one of the oldest ever discovered
The Tibesti Mountains include Emi Koussi, the highest peak in the Sahara at 3,415 m

name: Comoros
alphabetExample: Examples: ا, ب, ت — A, B, C
facts: An archipelago nicknamed the 'Perfumed Islands' for the ylang-ylang flowers used in perfumes
Has had over 20 coups or attempted coups since independence in 1975
Coelacanth — a 'living fossil' fish thought extinct for 65 million years — was rediscovered off these shores in 1938
One of the world's top producers of vanilla, cloves, and ylang-ylang
Mayotte, geographically part of the archipelago, voted to remain with France

name: Djibouti
alphabetExample: Examples: A, B, C — ا, ب, ت
facts: Lake Assal sits 155 m below sea level — the lowest point in Africa and the saltiest lake outside Antarctica
Hosts foreign military bases for the US, France, China, Japan, and Italy — strategic gateway to the Red Sea
Tiny but pivotal — controls the Bab-el-Mandeb strait, gateway between Indian Ocean and Red Sea
The Afar Triangle, where three tectonic plates meet, is slowly tearing the country apart
About 90% of the country is desert; the rest is sparse pasture

name: DR Congo
facts: Second-largest country in Africa by area — and the largest French-speaking country in the world by population
Holds about half of Africa's tropical rainforest — second only to the Amazon globally
The Congo River is the world's deepest river, with measured depths over 220 m
Home to bonobos, found nowhere else on Earth — humanity's closest living relative
Holds about 70% of the world's cobalt reserves, vital for lithium-ion batteries

name: Egypt
facts: The Great Pyramid is the only surviving Wonder of the Ancient World
Ancient Egyptians invented the 365-day calendar
Home to the Nile — the world's longest river
Cairo is the largest city in Africa and the Arab world
Ancient Egyptians used over 700 hieroglyphic symbols

name: Equatorial Guinea
facts: The only African country with Spanish as an official language
Independence from Spain in 1968 made it Africa's only Spanish-speaking former colony
Has one of the highest GDPs per capita in Africa thanks to oil — but income is highly unequal
Capital Malabo sits on the volcanic island of Bioko, separate from the mainland
Bioko Island has more primate species per square kilometre than almost anywhere on Earth

name: Eritrea
facts: Asmara is a UNESCO site — a perfectly preserved Italian Art Deco capital nicknamed 'La Piccola Roma'
Has nine officially recognized ethnic groups, each with its own language
One of only a few countries with no political parties — a one-party state since independence
Eritrea fought a 30-year war for independence from Ethiopia, finally winning in 1993
Home to the Dahlak Archipelago — pristine coral reefs barely touched by tourism

name: Eswatini
facts: Renamed from 'Swaziland' to 'Eswatini' in 2018, on the 50th anniversary of independence
One of the world's last absolute monarchies — King Mswati III holds full executive power
Hosts the colorful Umhlanga (Reed Dance) ceremony each year, drawing tens of thousands of unmarried women
Ngwenya Mine is one of the oldest known mines in the world — over 40,000 years
Smaller than New Jersey, but holds two UNESCO sites and three large game reserves

name: Ethiopia
facts: Oldest independent country in Africa
Coffee was discovered here — the word comes from the 'Kaffa' region
Has its own calendar with 13 months
Addis Ababa is the highest capital city in Africa
Lucy, one of humanity's oldest ancestors (3.2M years), was found here

name: Gabon
facts: About 88% of the country is covered by rainforest — and 11% is national parkland
Lopé National Park preserves rock art that's over 1,500 years old
Loango National Park is famous for hippos that swim in the Atlantic surf
Albert Schweitzer ran his Nobel-winning hospital in Lambaréné for over 50 years
Holds one of the largest natural nuclear-fission deposits ever found — at Oklo, around 1.7 billion years ago

name: Gambia
facts: Smallest country on the African mainland — a thin strip of land along the Gambia River
Almost entirely surrounded by Senegal, with only a short Atlantic coastline
Kunta Kinteh Island (formerly James Island) is a UNESCO site tied to the slave trade and Alex Haley's 'Roots'
More than 500 bird species — a top destination for birdwatchers in West Africa
Officially adds 'The' to its name to avoid confusion with Zambia in international listings

name: Ghana
facts: First sub-Saharan country to gain independence in 1957
One of the world's top gold producers
Lake Volta is the world's largest artificial lake by area
Coffin art is a celebrated tradition — planes, fish, and phones shaped coffins
Home to the legendary and powerful Ashanti Kingdom

name: Guinea
facts: Holds about a quarter of the world's known bauxite reserves — the ore for aluminum
Mount Nimba straddles three borders and is a UNESCO biosphere reserve
Headwaters of three of West Africa's largest rivers — the Niger, Senegal, and Gambia — rise here
Sékou Touré famously chose 'poverty in dignity' over French Community membership in 1958
Conakry, the capital, sits on the long, narrow Kaloum Peninsula in the Atlantic

name: Ivory Coast
facts: World's largest cocoa producer — supplies roughly 40% of global cacao
Yamoussoukro's Basilica of Our Lady of Peace is the largest church in the world by area
The economic capital Abidjan is sometimes called the 'Paris of West Africa'
Côte d'Ivoire officially asks the world to use only this French name — not 'Ivory Coast' translations
The Baoulé mask tradition of Côte d'Ivoire deeply influenced Picasso and 20th-century modern art

name: Kenya
facts: Home to the world's largest annual wildlife migration
Nairobi is one of only 3 cities with a national park inside city limits
Kenya produces world-class marathon runners due to high-altitude training
The Great Rift Valley runs through Kenya like a geological spine
The Maasai people measure wealth in cattle and children

name: Lesotho
facts: A landlocked enclave entirely surrounded by South Africa — one of only three such 'enclave countries' in the world
Has the highest 'low point' of any country — its lowest elevation is over 1,400 m above sea level
The Basotho blanket is national dress — worn even in summer as a status symbol
Africa's only ski resort, Afriski, sits in the Maluti Mountains at 3,222 m
Lesotho exports water to South Africa via the massive Highlands Water Project

name: Liberia
facts: Founded in 1822 by freed American slaves — the only African country never colonized by Europeans
Capital Monrovia is named after US President James Monroe — the only foreign capital named after a US president
Flag closely resembles the US flag — 11 stripes for the 11 signers of Liberia's declaration of independence
Home to one of West Africa's last remaining tracts of Upper Guinean rainforest
Ellen Johnson Sirleaf became Africa's first elected female head of state here in 2006

name: Libya
facts: Over 90% of Libya is covered by the Sahara Desert
Leptis Magna is one of the best-preserved Roman cities in the world — a UNESCO site
The Great Man-Made River is the world's largest irrigation project, pumping fossil water from Saharan aquifers
Holds Africa's largest proven oil reserves
Libya recorded one of the highest reliable temperatures on Earth — 58°C at Al 'Aziziyah in 1922 (later contested)

name: Madagascar
facts: About 90% of Madagascar's wildlife is found nowhere else on Earth — including all wild lemurs
Fourth-largest island in the world, separated from Africa around 165 million years ago
Avenue of the Baobabs features iconic 800-year-old Grandidier's baobabs
Vanilla — Madagascar's signature export — supplies most of the world's natural vanilla
Malagasy culture is closer to Southeast Asian roots — early settlers came from Borneo around 2,000 years ago

name: Malawi
facts: Lake Malawi has more fish species than any other lake on Earth — over 1,000, mostly cichlids
Often called the 'Warm Heart of Africa' for its famously friendly people
Lake Malawi is so big it produces its own weather, including powerful storms
Madonna's Raising Malawi foundation has built dozens of schools here
Mua Mission's Kungoni Centre houses one of Africa's finest collections of Chichewa wood carvings

name: Mali
facts: Timbuktu was a center of Islamic scholarship in the 14th–16th centuries with hundreds of thousands of manuscripts
Mansa Musa of Mali was likely the richest person who ever lived — his 1324 hajj distributed so much gold it caused inflation in Cairo for years
The Great Mosque of Djenné is the largest mud-brick building in the world
Music legends Salif Keïta, Ali Farka Touré, and Tinariwen come from Mali
Dogon Country is famed for cliff dwellings and intricate cosmological knowledge

name: Mauritania
facts: The 'Eye of the Sahara' (Richat Structure) — a 50 km circular geological feature visible from space
Last country in the world to officially abolish slavery — only criminalized in 2007
Around 75% of the country is desert, with the Sahara expanding southward each year
The Iron Ore Train from Zouérat to Nouadhibou is one of the longest trains on Earth — sometimes 3 km long
Banc d'Arguin National Park is a UNESCO site where millions of migrating shorebirds spend the winter

name: Mauritius
facts: Once home to the dodo — driven to extinction by Dutch sailors within a century of contact
Mark Twain wrote: 'God created Mauritius first, then made Heaven from the model'
First country in Africa to be ranked 'high human development' by the UN
Has a 'underwater waterfall' illusion off Le Morne — sand and silt running off the shelf create the optical effect
Mauritius issued the famous 'Post Office' stamps in 1847 — the rarest stamps in the world

name: Morocco
alphabet: Arabic and Tifinagh: ا, ⴰ, ⴱ
facts: Home to the world's oldest university — al-Qarawiyyin, founded 859 AD
One of only 3 countries with both Atlantic and Mediterranean coasts
Argan oil, the world's rarest, comes exclusively from Morocco
Traditional zellige tilework dates back to the 10th century
Morocco was among the first nations to recognize US independence

name: Mozambique
facts: Coastline of about 2,500 km — among the longest in Africa, dotted with archipelagos and reefs
The flag is the only national flag in the world featuring a modern firearm — an AK-47
Bazaruto Archipelago hosts one of the few remaining viable populations of dugongs in the Indian Ocean
Portuguese is the official language — Mozambique was a Portuguese colony until 1975
Mozambique's Mozambican Mfecane (warring period) reshaped southern Africa's tribal map in the 1800s

name: Namibia
facts: The Namib is the world's oldest desert — at least 55 million years old
Sossusvlei's red dunes include some over 300 m tall — among the highest on Earth
Skeleton Coast is named for the whale and shipwreck bones lining its shores
Namibia became the first country to specifically include environmental protection in its constitution
Cheetah capital of the world — Namibia hosts about a third of the global wild cheetah population

name: Niger
facts: About 80% of Niger lies in the Sahara — among the hottest countries on Earth
The Tree of Ténéré was once 'the most isolated tree on Earth' — knocked down by a truck in 1973
Holds about 5% of the world's uranium reserves — major supplier to French nuclear power
The Cure Salée festival sees Tuareg and Wodaabe nomads gather where the Niger River bends south
The 'Air and Ténéré' reserve is one of Africa's largest protected areas — twice the size of Switzerland

name: Nigeria
facts: Most populous country in Africa with 206 million people
Nollywood produces more films per year than Hollywood
Has over 500 distinct ethnic groups and languages
Lagos is Africa's largest city with over 20 million people
Nigeria is Africa's largest economy by GDP

name: Republic of the Congo
facts: Capital Brazzaville lies directly across the Congo River from Kinshasa — the closest capitals in the world (other than Vatican/Rome)
Around 70% of Republic of the Congo is covered in dense rainforest
Nouabalé-Ndoki National Park hosts one of the world's largest populations of western lowland gorillas
Was a French colony until 1960 — French is the official language
Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza, an Italian-French explorer, founded Brazzaville in 1880

name: Rwanda
facts: Known as the 'Land of a Thousand Hills' for its dramatic, terraced landscape
Plastic bags have been banned since 2008 — Kigali is one of the cleanest capitals on Earth
Volcanoes National Park is one of only three places where mountain gorillas can be visited in the wild
Has the highest percentage of women in parliament of any country — over 60%
Umuganda — community service held the last Saturday of each month — is required of all able citizens

name: São Tomé and Príncipe
facts: Smallest Portuguese-speaking country, made up of two volcanic islands in the Gulf of Guinea
Pico Cão Grande is a 370 m volcanic plug — one of the most striking on Earth
São Tomé sits almost exactly on the equator and the prime meridian
Cocoa is the islands' signature crop — world-famous for high-cacao single-origin chocolate
Independent from Portugal since 1975 — Africa's second-smallest country

name: Senegal
facts: Westernmost point of mainland Africa — Pointe des Almadies in Dakar
Lake Retba ('Lac Rose') turns vivid pink due to halophilic microbes and high salinity
The African Renaissance Monument in Dakar is the tallest statue in Africa at 49 m
Birthplace of poet-president Léopold Sédar Senghor and Négritude literary movement
Île de Gorée was a major slave-trading post — now a UNESCO memorial

name: Seychelles
facts: About half the country's land area is protected nature reserve
The coco de mer palm — endemic to Praslin's Vallée de Mai — produces the world's largest seed (up to 18 kg)
Aldabra Atoll, a UNESCO site, hosts the world's largest population of giant tortoises
Inner islands are granite — the only oceanic granitic islands on Earth, remnants of Gondwana
Population is about 100,000 — among the smallest of any African country

name: Sierra Leone
facts: Sierra Leone means 'Lion Mountains' in Portuguese — named by 15th-century explorer Pedro de Sintra
Capital Freetown was founded in 1792 by formerly enslaved Africans returning from North America and Britain
The 'Star of Sierra Leone' (1972) was one of the largest gem-quality diamonds ever found — 968.9 carats
Tiwai Island has one of the world's highest concentrations of primate species
About a third of the population belongs to the Mende ethnic group, with Temne the next largest

name: Somalia
alphabetExample: Examples: A, B, C — ا, ب, ت
facts: Has the longest coastline of any country in mainland Africa — over 3,300 km
Mogadishu was a major medieval Indian Ocean trading port — Ibn Battuta praised it in 1331
Camel population estimated at 7 million — the largest in the world
Frankincense and myrrh from the Cal Madow mountains were prized in the ancient world
The five-pointed star on the flag represents the five Somali-inhabited regions of the Horn of Africa

name: South Africa
language: Afrikaans, English, Southern Ndebele, Northern Sotho, Southern Sotho, Swazi, Tswana, Tsonga, Venda, Xhosa, Zulu
facts: Has 3 capital cities — one per branch of government
Produces 75% of the world's platinum
Has 11 official languages
The Cradle of Humankind has some of the oldest hominid fossils
Nelson Mandela was imprisoned for 27 years before becoming president

name: South Sudan
facts: World's youngest country — gained independence from Sudan on 9 July 2011
Boma–Jonglei is one of Africa's largest mammal migrations — millions of antelope and gazelle
The Sudd is one of the world's largest wetlands — a vast swamp on the White Nile
Has more than 60 indigenous languages, with English chosen as the official language at independence
The Dinka, the largest ethnic group, are known for elaborate cattle-keeping traditions

name: Sudan
facts: Has more pyramids than Egypt — about 200 Nubian pyramids at Meroë and other sites
The Blue and White Nile meet at Khartoum to form the main Nile
Was Africa's largest country until South Sudan split off in 2011 — now third-largest
Sudan's Nubian rulers (the 'Black Pharaohs') ruled all of Egypt as the 25th Dynasty around 750 BC
The Sahara Desert covers most of the north, while the south is savanna and swamp

name: Tanzania
facts: Mount Kilimanjaro is Africa's highest peak at 5,895 m — and the world's tallest free-standing mountain
The Serengeti hosts the world's largest land migration — about 2 million wildebeest, zebra, and gazelle each year
Olduvai Gorge holds some of the oldest human ancestor fossils ever found
Zanzibar's Stone Town is a UNESCO site at the cultural crossroads of Africa, Arabia, and India
Lake Tanganyika, Tanzania's western border, is the world's second-deepest and second-oldest lake

name: Togo
facts: One of Africa's narrowest countries — about 50 km wide along its main coastline
Koutammakou's Tata Somba mud-tower houses are a UNESCO World Heritage site
Lake Togo's stilted village Togoville is the spiritual heart of voodoo in the country
Was a German colony from 1884 to 1914 — split between France and Britain after WWI
Phosphate is Togo's biggest mining export, used in fertilizer worldwide

name: Tunisia
facts: Carthage, near Tunis, was Rome's great rival — destroyed in 146 BC after the Punic Wars
El Djem holds one of the largest and best-preserved Roman amphitheaters in the world
The 2010–2011 Jasmine Revolution started in Tunisia and sparked the Arab Spring
Star Wars filmed Tatooine scenes in southern Tunisian deserts — including Luke's home
First Arab country to grant women the right to vote (1957) and ban polygamy (1956)

name: Uganda
facts: Lake Victoria — shared with Kenya and Tanzania — is Africa's largest lake and the source of the White Nile
Home to about half of the world's mountain gorillas, mostly in Bwindi Impenetrable Forest
Often called the 'Pearl of Africa' — a phrase popularized by Winston Churchill
About half the population is under 15 — among the youngest median ages on Earth
Crosses the equator — the line is marked by a popular monument near Kayabwe

name: Zambia
facts: Victoria Falls (shared with Zimbabwe) is one of the largest waterfalls on Earth — over 1.7 km wide
Devil's Pool at the lip of Victoria Falls lets brave swimmers peer over the edge
Around 30% of Zambia's land is dedicated to national parks, reserves, and game management areas
Copper is the country's main export — Zambia is one of the world's top producers
Lake Kariba (shared with Zimbabwe) is the world's largest reservoir by volume

name: Zimbabwe
facts: Great Zimbabwe was a powerful medieval city of stone — the largest pre-colonial structure in sub-Saharan Africa
Victoria Falls — locally Mosi-oa-Tunya, 'the smoke that thunders' — is a UNESCO site shared with Zambia
Hyperinflation in 2008 led to a 100 trillion-dollar Zimbabwe banknote
Hwange National Park is home to Africa's second-largest elephant population
Has 16 official languages — among the most of any country

name: Afghanistan
alphabetExample: Arabic script examples: الف, ب, ت
facts: Lapis lazuli has been mined here for over 6,000 years
Buzkashi uses a goat carcass — the thrilling national sport
Home to the Hindu Kush, one of the world's highest mountain ranges
Produces over 80% of the world's illicit opium
The giant Buddhas of Bamiyan were carved in the 6th century AD

name: Armenia
facts: First nation in the world to adopt Christianity as a state religion — in 301 AD
Mount Ararat, sacred to Armenians, is now in Turkey but appears on Armenia's coat of arms
Has its own unique alphabet — created by Mesrop Mashtots in 405 AD
Lake Sevan, at 1,900 m, is one of the largest high-altitude freshwater lakes in the world
Armenian khachkars (cross-stones) are a UNESCO-listed sculpture tradition unique to the country

name: Azerbaijan
facts: Known as the 'Land of Fire' — natural gas seeps have burned continuously for thousands of years at Yanardağ
Baku's medieval walled Old City is a UNESCO site, alongside the futuristic Flame Towers
First country in the Muslim world to grant women the vote — 1918
Mud volcanoes — about half the world's total — are scattered across the country
Sits on the Caspian Sea, the world's largest inland body of water

name: Bahrain
facts: An archipelago of about 33 islands — but most of the population lives on the main island
First place in the Persian Gulf to discover oil, in 1932
The Tree of Life, a 9-meter mesquite, has thrived in the desert for over 400 years with no obvious water source
Bahrain hosts the first Formula 1 race of the season at the Sakhir circuit
Was a center of the ancient Dilmun civilization — mentioned in Sumerian texts as 'paradise'

name: Bangladesh
facts: World's most densely populated large country — over 170 million people in an area smaller than Iowa
The Sundarbans is the largest mangrove forest on Earth and home to the rare Bengal tiger
Site of the Bengal famine of 1943 and the 1971 war that established its independence from Pakistan
Cox's Bazar is one of the longest natural sea beaches in the world — about 120 km long
Muhammad Yunus and Grameen Bank pioneered microfinance — winning the Nobel Peace Prize in 2006

name: Bhutan
facts: Measures success by Gross National Happiness instead of GDP — written into its constitution
Last country to introduce television and the internet — only allowed in 1999
Carbon-negative — its forests absorb more CO₂ than the country emits
Tiger's Nest Monastery clings to a cliff 900 m above the Paro Valley
Plastic bags banned since 1999; tourists pay a Sustainable Development Fee per day

name: Brunei
alphabetExample: Examples: A, B, C — ا, ب, ت
facts: One of the world's wealthiest countries per capita thanks to oil and gas
The Sultan's Istana Nurul Iman is the world's largest residential palace, with 1,788 rooms
Kampong Ayer is a 'water village' of 30,000 people on stilts — the largest of its kind
About 70% of the country is covered in pristine rainforest
Brunei is split in two by Malaysia's Sarawak state

name: Cambodia
alphabet: Khmer script: ក, ខ, គ
facts: Angkor Wat is the largest religious monument in the world — over 162 hectares
The Khmer Empire ruled most of mainland Southeast Asia from the 9th to 15th centuries
Tonle Sap Lake reverses its flow direction twice a year with the monsoon
Cambodia's flag is the only national flag in the world depicting a building (Angkor Wat)
Cambodian script is one of the oldest alphabets still in use, dating to the 7th century

name: China
alphabet: Chinese characters: 中, 国, 人
facts: The Great Wall stretches over 21,000 km
China invented paper, printing, gunpowder, and the compass
Giant pandas are only found wild in Sichuan province
Uses only one time zone despite spanning five geographical ones
The Forbidden City has 9,999 rooms — one short of heavenly perfection

name: Georgia
facts: One of the world's oldest wine regions — winemaking dates back over 8,000 years
Has its own unique alphabet — Mkhedruli, created in the 11th century
Stalin was born in Georgia — in the town of Gori in 1878
Vardzia is a 12th-century cave city carved into a mountainside
Stretches from the Black Sea to the high Caucasus — Mount Shkhara reaches 5,201 m

name: India
facts: World's largest democracy with 1.4 billion people
Chess was invented in India around the 6th century AD
Has 22 official languages and hundreds of dialects
The Taj Mahal took 22 years and 22,000 workers to build
Produces more films than any other country on Earth

name: Indonesia
facts: World's largest archipelago with over 17,000 islands
Has the highest marine biodiversity on Earth
Komodo dragons, the world's largest lizards, live only here
The 1883 Krakatoa eruption was heard 4,800 km away
Has more active volcanoes than any other country

name: Iran
facts: One of the world's oldest civilizations at over 7,000 years
World's largest producer of saffron
Persian poetry is considered among the finest ever written
Sits on 10% of the world's proven oil reserves
Invented the concept of the postal system in 550 BC

name: Iraq
facts: Mesopotamia — the 'land between the rivers' — is the cradle of civilization, urbanization and writing
Sumerians invented cuneiform writing here over 5,000 years ago
The Hanging Gardens of Babylon were one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
The Marsh Arabs of southern Iraq live on floating reed islands — a 5,000-year-old way of life
Hammurabi's Code (~1754 BC), one of the oldest written legal codes, was created here

name: Israel
facts: Jerusalem is sacred to three major religions — Judaism, Christianity, and Islam
The Dead Sea is the lowest point on dry land at about 430 m below sea level
Israel has the highest number of museums per capita in the world
Hebrew is the only ancient language successfully revived as a daily spoken language
About a tenth of the country is desert (the Negev) but Israel exports food thanks to drip irrigation, invented here

name: Japan
alphabet: Kanji, Hiragana, Katakana: あ, い, 漢
facts: Has the world's oldest company — Kongō Gumi, founded 578 AD
Experiences about 1,500 earthquakes every year
More vending machines per person than any other country
Japan's rail system has an average delay of just 54 seconds
Mount Fuji is climbed by over 300,000 people annually

name: Jordan
facts: Petra, carved into rose-red cliffs by the Nabataeans, is one of the New Seven Wonders of the World
Wadi Rum's red sands have stood in for Mars and the desert planets in many Hollywood films
The Dead Sea — lowest point on Earth — sits on Jordan's western border
Jordan has more refugees per capita than almost any other country
Roman Jerash is one of the largest and best-preserved Roman provincial cities outside Italy

name: Kazakhstan
alphabet: Cyrillic and (now) Latin: А, Б, В
facts: Largest landlocked country in the world — bigger than Western Europe
Baikonur Cosmodrome — leased to Russia — is the world's first and largest space-launch facility
Apples are believed to have originated in Kazakhstan — wild Malus sieversii is the ancestor of all cultivated apples
The Aral Sea, on the border with Uzbekistan, has shrunk to about a tenth of its former size — one of the worst environmental disasters
Kazakhs traditionally hunt with golden eagles — a 6,000-year-old practice

name: Kuwait
facts: Kuwait Towers are an iconic skyline of three tapered concrete towers, with two storing 9,000 m³ of water
Has the world's third-largest sovereign wealth fund — valued in the hundreds of billions
Burned for nine months — Iraq's retreating army set fire to about 700 oil wells in 1991
The Kuwaiti dinar is the highest-valued currency in the world
Kuwait City sits at the head of the Persian Gulf, an ancient trading port called 'Qurayn'

name: Kyrgyzstan
facts: About 90% of the country lies above 1,500 m — one of the most mountainous nations on Earth
Lake Issyk-Kul is the world's second-largest mountain lake — saline and never freezes
The yurt is a national symbol — represented even in the center of the flag (the sun's rays)
Manas, the Kyrgyz national epic, is one of the world's longest poems — 20 times longer than the Iliad
Eagle hunting on horseback (with golden eagles) has been practiced for over 4,000 years here

name: Laos
alphabet: Lao script: ກ, ຂ, ຄ
facts: The only landlocked country in Southeast Asia
The Plain of Jars is dotted with thousands of mysterious 2,000-year-old stone vessels
Heavily bombed during the Vietnam War — Laos remains the most-bombed country per capita in history
Luang Prabang's old town, with hundreds of monks in saffron robes at dawn, is a UNESCO site
Khone Phapheng on the Mekong is the largest waterfall by volume in Southeast Asia

name: Lebanon
facts: Beirut has been destroyed and rebuilt seven times — earning it the nickname 'the Phoenix City'
The cedar tree on the flag is the symbol of Lebanon — sacred since biblical times
Has 18 officially recognized religious sects — the political system explicitly balances them
Byblos is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world — perhaps over 7,000 years
Lebanon has more journalists per capita than almost any country, and a famously free press

name: Malaysia
facts: Petronas Twin Towers were the world's tallest buildings from 1998 to 2004 and remain the tallest twin towers
Has one of the oldest rainforests on Earth — the Taman Negara forest is around 130 million years old
Borneo's Mount Kinabalu, in Sabah, is Southeast Asia's highest peak between the Himalayas and Papua
Rafflesia arnoldii, the world's largest single flower, blooms in Malaysian forests — up to a metre across
Malaysia is split between the peninsula and the northern third of Borneo

name: Maldives
facts: World's lowest country — average ground level is only 1.5 m above sea level
Has 1,192 coral islands grouped into 26 atolls in the Indian Ocean
Held the world's first underwater cabinet meeting in 2009 to highlight climate threats
Population is around 540,000 across only about 200 inhabited islands
Languages, scripts and culture have Sinhalese, Arabic, and Indian roots — Dhivehi is unique to the country

name: Mongolia
alphabetExample: Examples: А, Б, В — ᠠ, ᠡ
facts: World's least densely populated sovereign country — about 2 people per km²
Genghis Khan's empire (founded 1206) became the largest contiguous land empire in history
About 30% of Mongolians still live a nomadic or semi-nomadic life in gers (yurts)
Naadam festival celebrates the 'Three Manly Sports': wrestling, archery, and horse racing
Ulaanbaatar is the world's coldest capital city, averaging −1.3 °C annually

name: Myanmar
alphabet: Burmese script: က, ခ, ဂ
facts: Bagan plain holds over 2,000 ancient Buddhist temples and stupas across 100 km²
Officially renamed from Burma to Myanmar in 1989 — both names are still used
The Kayan 'long-neck' women wear brass coils that lengthen their apparent neck from childhood
Capital was moved from Yangon to the purpose-built Naypyidaw in 2005 — much of it remains nearly empty
Inle Lake's fishermen famously row standing on one leg

name: Nepal
facts: Home to 8 of the world's 10 highest mountains — including Everest at 8,849 m
The only country with a non-rectangular flag — two stacked triangles representing the Himalayas
Birthplace of Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha) — Lumbini is a UNESCO site
Never colonized — uniquely retained independence among South Asian nations
Has its own time zone offset by 5:45 from UTC — one of only a few countries with a 45-minute offset

name: North Korea
facts: Uses its own calendar system — Juche — counting from Kim Il-sung's birth year (1912 = Juche 1)
The 38th Parallel divides the Korean Peninsula since the Korean War armistice in 1953
Pyongyang's metro is the world's deepest, doubling as a nuclear shelter
The Mass Games at Rungrado Stadium can feature over 100,000 performers in choreographed routines
Among the world's most isolated countries, with most internet limited to a domestic intranet (Kwangmyong)

name: Oman
facts: Frankincense from southern Oman supplied the ancient world — the 'Land of Frankincense' is a UNESCO site
Oldest independent state in the Arab world — never formally colonized
Bahla Fort and the falaj irrigation channels are among Oman's many UNESCO sites
The Empty Quarter (Rub' al Khali), the largest sand desert on Earth, extends into Oman
Has an exclave on the Strait of Hormuz, separated from the rest by the UAE

name: Pakistan
facts: Indus Valley Civilization (Mohenjo-daro, Harappa) flourished here ~4,500 years ago — among Earth's earliest cities
K2, the world's second-highest mountain at 8,611 m, sits on Pakistan's border with China
World's largest deep-sea port construction is at Gwadar, a strategic Arabian Sea harbor
Has the world's largest deep-sea earthen dam — Tarbela on the Indus
Cricket is a national passion — Pakistan won the 1992 ICC Cricket World Cup

name: Palestine
currency: Egyptian pound, Israeli new shekel, Jordanian dinar
facts: Bethlehem and the Church of the Nativity, traditional birthplace of Jesus, is a UNESCO site
Hebron's Old City has been continuously inhabited for over 5,000 years
The West Bank's Wadi Qelt and Mar Saba Monastery host Christian hermitic traditions over 1,500 years old
Recognized as a non-member observer state at the United Nations since 2012
Olive oil is central to Palestinian cuisine and economy — some olive trees in the West Bank are over 4,000 years old

name: Philippines
facts: An archipelago of over 7,640 islands in the western Pacific
Has the second-largest English-speaking population in Asia after India
Mount Pinatubo's 1991 eruption was the second-largest of the 20th century, cooling Earth by about 0.5 °C for two years
Tubbataha Reefs Natural Park is one of the world's most biodiverse marine sites
The jeepney — a flamboyant minibus made from WWII surplus jeeps — is a Filipino icon

name: Qatar
facts: Hosted the 2022 FIFA World Cup — the first held in the Middle East
World's largest exporter of liquefied natural gas (LNG)
Doha's Museum of Islamic Art was designed by I. M. Pei — his last major commission
The smallest population of any World Cup host nation — about 3 million people
Camel racing in Qatar has used robot jockeys since 2005

name: Saudi Arabia
facts: Home to Mecca, Islam's holiest city, visited by 2+ million yearly
Has 25% of the world's known oil reserves
The Empty Quarter is the world's largest continuous sand desert
Has no rivers — only dry seasonal wadis
Women were only allowed to drive from June 2018

name: Singapore
alphabetExample: Examples: A, B, C — 中, 国, 人 — அ, ஆ, இ
facts: One of only three modern city-states in the world
Has more than 200 km of underground rail despite its small size
Changi Airport has been ranked the world's best airport over a dozen times
Singapore's Supertree Grove and Cloud Forest are biomimicry icons of urban greening
Has more than four official languages: English, Mandarin, Malay, and Tamil

name: South Korea
facts: Has the world's fastest average internet speeds
K-pop and K-drama are now global cultural phenomena
Koreans consider themselves 1 year old at birth
Seoul is one of the world's 10 largest cities
Went from war-torn poverty to top-20 economy in just 50 years

name: Sri Lanka
facts: World's largest exporter of Ceylon tea — long renowned as one of the finest black teas
Sigiriya, the 'Lion Rock', is a 5th-century palace and fortress on a 200 m monolith
Adam's Peak holds a sacred footprint claimed by Buddhists, Hindus, Christians and Muslims
One of only a few countries with eight UNESCO World Heritage sites despite its small size
Sri Lanka was previously known as Ceylon — renamed in 1972

name: Syria
facts: Damascus is among the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world — perhaps 11,000 years
Aleppo's Old City and the Crusader castle Krak des Chevaliers are UNESCO sites
Syria includes Ugarit, where the world's earliest known alphabet was developed around 1400 BC
Palmyra, an ancient caravan oasis, was a hub of trade between Rome and the East
The Euphrates River, vital for agriculture, flows through Syria from Turkey toward Iraq

name: Tajikistan
facts: About 93% of Tajikistan is covered by mountains — the Pamirs are nicknamed the 'Roof of the World'
Pik Ismoil Somoni reaches 7,495 m — the highest peak in the former USSR
The Pamir Highway is one of the highest international roads in the world
Speaks Tajik, a variety of Persian written in Cyrillic script
Iskanderkul, a turquoise lake, is named after Alexander the Great ('Iskander')

name: Thailand
alphabet: Thai script: ก, ข, ค
facts: Never colonized by a European power — the only SE Asian nation
World's largest exporter of rice and natural rubber
Bangkok's full ceremonial name is 168 characters long
Has over 40,000 Buddhist temples
The number 9 is very lucky — the Thai word sounds like 'progress'

name: Timor-Leste
facts: One of the youngest countries — gained independence from Indonesia in 2002
Eastern half of the island of Timor; the western half belongs to Indonesia
Portuguese is an official language — a legacy of nearly 500 years of Portuguese rule
Coffee is the country's biggest export crop, mostly Arabica grown organically
The Tatamailau (Mount Ramelau) at 2,986 m is the country's highest peak

name: Turkmenistan
facts: The 'Door to Hell' — Darvaza gas crater — has been burning continuously since 1971
About 80% of the country is covered by the Karakum Desert
Ashgabat is in the Guinness Book of Records for the highest concentration of white-marble buildings
The Akhal-Teke horse, often called 'the heavenly horse', is a national symbol on the coat of arms
Holds among the world's largest reserves of natural gas

name: United Arab Emirates
facts: Burj Khalifa in Dubai is the world's tallest building at 828 m
A federation of seven emirates, formed in 1971 — Abu Dhabi is the capital
About 90% of UAE residents are foreign nationals
Built the world's largest indoor ski slope and several artificial palm-shaped islands
Was the first Arab nation to send a probe to Mars — the Hope probe arrived in February 2021

name: Uzbekistan
facts: Samarkand and Bukhara were grand Silk Road cities — both UNESCO sites
Doubly landlocked — surrounded only by other landlocked countries (along with Liechtenstein)
Tashkent's metro stations are decorated like underground palaces — among the most beautiful in the world
Holds the world's largest open-pit gold mine, at Muruntau
The shrinking Aral Sea (shared with Kazakhstan) is one of the world's biggest environmental disasters

name: Vietnam
alphabet: Latin alphabet with diacritics: A, Â, Ă
facts: World's largest exporter of cashews
Ha Long Bay has 2,000+ limestone islands rising from emerald waters
Son Doong is the world's largest cave with its own weather system
2nd largest coffee producer in the world after Brazil
One of the few remaining communist-governed countries

name: Yemen
facts: Socotra Island is so isolated that about a third of its plants — including the Dragon's Blood Tree — exist nowhere else
Sana'a's Old City is over 2,500 years old — its mud-brick towers are a UNESCO site
Coffee was first commercialized in Yemen — the port of Mocha gave its name to the drink
Wadi Hadhramaut's Shibam is a 16th-century 'Manhattan of the desert' of mud-brick high-rises
Among the oldest centers of civilization in the Arabian Peninsula — home to the Sabaean Kingdom (Queen of Sheba)

name: Albania
facts: Nodding means 'no' and shaking means 'yes' — the exact opposite!
Has 750,000 bunkers — one for every 4 citizens
The double-headed eagle on the flag dates back 600 years
Mother Teresa was of Albanian heritage
Blood feuds under the Kanun code occasionally persist today

name: Andorra
facts: Microstate in the Pyrenees, governed jointly by the President of France and the Bishop of Urgell (Spain) since 1278
Has the longest life expectancy in the European region
Catalan is the only official language — Andorra is the only country where it's the sole official tongue
No income tax until 2015 — long a haven for shoppers and tax refugees
Andorra has no airport or railway — the only way in is by road or helicopter

name: Austria
facts: Mozart, Beethoven, Schubert, Strauss, and Mahler all worked in Vienna — the world's classical-music capital
Has won more Winter Olympic medals per capita than any other country
Vienna has been ranked the world's most liveable city multiple years running
The Sound of Music is set in Salzburg — though most Austrians have never seen it
About 60% of Austria is in the Alps — Großglockner is the highest peak at 3,798 m

name: Belarus
facts: About 40% of Belarus is forested — Białowieża Forest is one of Europe's last primeval woods, home to wild bison
Has the world's largest population of European bison after centuries of near-extinction
Most of Belarus's population speaks Russian, even though Belarusian is also official
Was the most devastated Soviet republic in WWII — about a quarter of its population died
Brest Fortress's defense in 1941 became one of the war's most famous resistance stories

name: Belgium
facts: Brussels hosts the headquarters of the European Union and NATO
Belgium produces over 1,000 different beers — including world-class Trappist abbey ales
Invented the saxophone (Adolphe Sax, 1846) and Belgian fries — yes, fries are Belgian, not French
Comic-strip culture is huge — Tintin, the Smurfs, and Lucky Luke are all Belgian
Has had three official languages since 1993: Dutch, French, and German

name: Bosnia and Herzegovina
alphabetExample: Examples: A, B, C — А, Б, В
facts: Sarajevo's main library was deliberately shelled in 1992 — destroying centuries of Bosnian heritage
The 1984 Winter Olympics were held in Sarajevo, just before the Yugoslav Wars
The Old Bridge of Mostar — destroyed in 1993 — was painstakingly rebuilt and is a UNESCO site
The country has three constituent peoples (Bosniaks, Serbs, Croats) and a rotating tripartite presidency
Stećci — medieval tombstones unique to the western Balkans — are a UNESCO site shared with neighbours

name: Bulgaria
facts: Bulgaria gave the world the Cyrillic alphabet — created by saints Cyril and Methodius' disciples in the 9th century
Has one of the oldest known gold treasures, the Varna Necropolis, around 6,500 years old
Rose oil from the Valley of Roses near Kazanlak supplies most of the world's perfume industry
Yogurt's signature culture, Lactobacillus bulgaricus, is named after Bulgaria
Plovdiv is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in Europe — over 6,000 years old

name: Croatia
facts: Has more than 1,200 islands along the Adriatic coast
Plitvice Lakes is a UNESCO site of 16 terraced lakes connected by waterfalls
Game of Thrones' King's Landing was filmed largely in Dubrovnik's medieval walls
The necktie was invented in Croatia — Croatian mercenaries' neckwear inspired Louis XIII's 'cravat'
Tesla was born in Smiljan in 1856 — to Serbian parents in what is today Croatia

name: Cyprus
alphabetExample: Examples: Α, Β, Γ — A, B, C
facts: Mythological birthplace of Aphrodite, Greek goddess of love
Has been divided since 1974 — the Republic of Cyprus controls the south, while the north is the unrecognized TRNC
Halloumi, the squeaky cheese, has EU Protected Designation of Origin status here
Nicosia is the world's last divided capital — split by the UN-administered Green Line
Cyprus has more sunny days than almost anywhere in the Mediterranean — about 326 a year

name: Czechia
alphabet: Latin with diacritics: A, Á, Č
facts: Officially renamed 'Czechia' as the short-form name in 2016 (still 'Czech Republic' formally)
Czechs drink the most beer per capita in the world — around 140 litres a year
Prague's Old Town has been continuously inhabited for over 1,100 years
Pilsner-style beer was invented in Plzeň in 1842 — the original 'pilsner'
Charles University in Prague (1348) is the oldest university in central Europe

name: Denmark
alphabet: Latin alphabet plus Æ, Ø, Å
facts: Lego was invented in Billund, Denmark, in 1932 — the name comes from Danish 'leg godt' ('play well')
Consistently ranks among the happiest countries in the world
Greenland and the Faroe Islands are autonomous parts of the Kingdom of Denmark
Has the oldest continuously used flag — the Dannebrog dates to at least 1219
Danes invented LEGO, the Bluetooth name (after Harald Bluetooth), and the language structure of HTML's CMS Umbraco

name: Estonia
alphabet: Latin alphabet plus Õ, Ä, Ö, Ü
facts: World's most digital society — Estonia introduced e-residency in 2014, the first country to do so
About half the country is covered in forest — and bogs cover roughly a fifth
Skype was developed in Estonia and launched in 2003
Singing Revolution: massive song-festival protests helped Estonia regain independence in 1991
Tallinn's medieval Old Town is among Europe's best-preserved walled cities — a UNESCO site

name: Finland
alphabet: Latin alphabet plus Å, Ä, Ö
facts: Has more saunas (about 2 million) than cars
About 188,000 lakes dot the country — the 'Land of a Thousand Lakes' is an undercount
Hosts the Wife-Carrying World Championships and the Air Guitar World Championships every year
Long ranked the world's happiest country in the UN World Happiness Report
Finnish, with its 15 noun cases, is unrelated to Swedish or Russian — it's a Uralic language

name: France
facts: Most visited country on Earth with 90 million tourists per year
Has 246 types of cheese — one for every day
The Eiffel Tower grows 6 inches taller in summer from heat expansion
French was the world's diplomatic language until the 20th century
The Louvre is the world's most visited art museum

name: Germany
facts: Germany has 1,500 different types of beer
The printing press was invented by Gutenberg in Germany in 1440
Has the 4th largest economy in the world
There are over 20,000 castles across Germany
The first magazine in history launched in Germany in 1663

name: Greece
facts: Has over 6,000 islands but only 227 are inhabited
The Olympics were born in ancient Greece in 776 BC
Longest coastline in the Mediterranean Sea
More archaeological museums per capita than any other country
The Greek alphabet is the ancestor of Latin and Cyrillic scripts

name: Hungary
alphabet: Latin with diacritics: A, Á, Ö, Ű
facts: Budapest is built on top of more than 100 thermal springs — a 'spa capital' since Roman times
The Rubik's Cube was invented in 1974 by Hungarian architect Ernő Rubik
Has produced 13 Nobel laureates — extraordinary for a country of about 9.6 million
Hungarian (Magyar) is unrelated to its neighbours — its closest cousins are Finnish and Estonian
The country's parliament building, on the Danube, has 691 rooms and 20 km of stairs

name: Iceland
facts: Powered almost entirely by renewable energy — about 85% from geothermal and hydropower
Has no standing army — but does have a coast guard
Iceland's parliament, the Althingi, was founded in 930 AD — the oldest in the world still functioning
Around 60% of Icelanders believe (or won't deny) in elves and 'hidden people'
Reykjavík is the world's northernmost capital city

name: Ireland
facts: Has more sheep than people — about 5 million sheep to ~5 million people
St Patrick, Ireland's patron saint, was actually born in Roman Britain
Ireland's literature has produced four Nobel laureates: Yeats, Shaw, Beckett, and Heaney
Newgrange in County Meath is older than both the Pyramids and Stonehenge
The harp on Irish coins is the only national emblem in the world that's a musical instrument

name: Italy
facts: Has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any other country (58)
Invented pizza, pasta, and espresso
The Vatican inside Rome is the world's smallest country
Ancient Rome gave the world law, roads, and aqueducts
Italian is the closest living language to classical Latin

name: Latvia
alphabet: Latin alphabet plus Ā, Č, Š, Ž
facts: About half of Latvia is forest — wolves, lynx, and bears still roam there
Riga's Art Nouveau architecture is one of the densest collections in Europe — a UNESCO site
The Latvian song festival, held since 1873, gathers over 30,000 singers
Latvian is one of only two surviving Baltic languages (with Lithuanian)
Latvia has one of the highest broadband-internet speeds in the world

name: Liechtenstein
facts: Doubly landlocked — bordered only by Switzerland and Austria, themselves landlocked
World's largest exporter of false teeth, made by Ivoclar Vivadent in Schaan
World's largest producer of postage stamps per capita
The world's last country named after the family that still owns it — the Liechtenstein dynasty
Has no airport — the closest is in Zürich, Switzerland

name: Lithuania
alphabet: Latin alphabet plus Ą, Č, Ę, Š, Ū
facts: First Soviet republic to declare independence from the USSR (March 1990)
Vilnius's Old Town is one of the largest medieval old towns in Northern Europe
Lithuania was the largest country in Europe in the 15th century — stretching from the Baltic to the Black Sea
Hill of Crosses near Šiauliai contains over 100,000 crosses left by pilgrims
Holds the Guinness record for the largest amber forgery — and the largest amber stone, 'Sun Stone'

name: Luxembourg
facts: World's only Grand Duchy — ruled by a Grand Duke
Has the highest GDP per capita of any country in the world
Luxembourg City's old quarters and fortifications are a UNESCO site
Trilingual education is standard — children learn Luxembourgish, French, and German in school
Free public transport throughout the country since 2020 — the first nation to do so

name: Malta
alphabet: Latin plus Ċ, Ġ, Ħ, Ż
facts: Megalithic temples in Malta — Ġgantija on Gozo — are older than the Pyramids and Stonehenge
Has three official languages: Maltese, English, and Maltese Sign Language
Maltese is the only Semitic language written in the Latin alphabet — descended from medieval Sicilian Arabic
Around the rocky coast: Malta has the world's deepest-recorded saltwater swimming hole, the Blue Hole
Knights of Malta — the Sovereign Military Order of Malta — still exists with diplomatic status

name: Moldova
alphabet: Latin alphabet plus Ă, Â, Î, Ș, Ț
facts: Cricova's underground wine cellars stretch over 120 km — among the largest wine cellars in the world
Wine makes up around 3% of Moldova's GDP — one of the highest shares globally
Transnistria, a breakaway region, prints its own money and has its own flag — but is unrecognized
Moldovan and Romanian are essentially the same language
Until 1991, Moldova was part of the Soviet Union as the Moldavian SSR

name: Monaco
facts: Second-smallest country on Earth — and the most densely populated
Has won more Olympic medals than any country with no Olympic athletes from before 1924
Monte Carlo's casino has been an icon of high-stakes gambling since 1863
Hosts the Monaco Grand Prix — the most glamorous race in Formula 1
Ruled by the Grimaldi family since 1297 — one of Europe's oldest dynasties

name: Montenegro
alphabetExample: Examples: A, B, C — А, Б, В
facts: Name means 'Black Mountain' — a translation of Crna Gora
Among the youngest countries in Europe — gained independence from Serbia in 2006
Bay of Kotor is sometimes called Europe's southernmost fjord (technically a ria)
Tara River Canyon is the deepest canyon in Europe at 1,300 m
Adopted the euro despite not being a member of the EU or eurozone

name: Netherlands
facts: One-third of the Netherlands is below sea level
Amsterdam has more bicycles than people
Has more canals than Venice
The Dutch Golden Age (17th century) made them the richest nation
Tulips are not native to the Netherlands — they came from Central Asia

name: North Macedonia
facts: Renamed from 'Macedonia' to 'North Macedonia' in 2019 to settle a dispute with Greece
Lake Ohrid (shared with Albania) is one of Europe's deepest and oldest lakes — over 1.4 million years old
Mother Teresa was born Anjezë Bojaxhiu in Skopje in 1910
Skopje's enormous statues (the 'Skopje 2014' project) drew global attention for their sheer number
Holds about 1,000 archaeological sites — many tied to ancient Macedonia and the Roman empire

name: Norway
facts: Has the world's longest road tunnel at 24.5 km
Discovered oil in 1969, transforming from poor fishing nation to one of richest
Some Norwegians experience 76 days of continuous midnight sun
Invented the paper clip and the cheese slicer
Norway's sovereign wealth fund is the world's largest at over $1.3 trillion

name: Poland
alphabet: Latin alphabet plus Ą, Ć, Ę, Ł, Ń, Ó, Ś, Ź, Ż
facts: Białowieża Forest — shared with Belarus — is one of the last primeval forests in Europe
Marie Curie, the first person to win two Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, was born in Warsaw
Has the largest castle by land area in the world — Malbork Castle, built by Teutonic Knights
Wieliczka Salt Mine has been worked since the 13th century and includes underground chapels carved from salt
Pope John Paul II — born Karol Wojtyła — was Polish; the first non-Italian pope in over 450 years

name: Portugal
facts: Oldest nation-state in Europe with the same defined borders since 1139
Portuguese is the most widely spoken language in the Southern Hemisphere — over 250 million speakers
Portugal launched the Age of Discovery — Vasco da Gama, Magellan, and Henry the Navigator were Portuguese
Cork: about half of the world's cork comes from Portugal's cork-oak forests
The University of Coimbra — founded 1290 — is one of the oldest universities in Europe

name: Romania
alphabet: Latin alphabet plus Ă, Â, Î, Ș, Ț
facts: Bran Castle in Transylvania is popularly tied to Bram Stoker's Dracula (and Vlad the Impaler)
The Carpathian Mountains hold Europe's largest population of brown bears
Romanian is the only Romance language in Eastern Europe — closer to Latin than its neighbours
Bucharest's Palace of the Parliament is the heaviest building in the world — and the second-largest by area
Transfăgărășan road, called by Top Gear 'the best road in the world', climbs to 2,042 m

name: Russia
facts: Largest country on Earth spanning 11 time zones
Lake Baikal holds 20% of the world's unfrozen surface freshwater
Trans-Siberian Railway is the world's longest at 9,288 km
Moscow's metro is famous for its ornate, palace-like stations
Russia has 23 UNESCO World Heritage Sites

name: San Marino
facts: Claims to be the world's oldest republic — founded in 301 AD
Tiny enclave entirely surrounded by Italy
Has two democratically elected heads of state — the Captains Regent — who serve six-month terms
Stamps and coins are major exports for collectors
Hosts car races including the San Marino Grand Prix (held at Imola, in Italy)

name: Serbia
alphabetExample: Examples: А, Б, В — A, B, C
facts: Belgrade sits at the confluence of the Sava and Danube rivers — one of Europe's most strategic spots
Nikola Tesla was a Serbian-born inventor — Belgrade's main airport is named after him
Produces about a third of the world's raspberries
Serbia uses both Cyrillic and Latin scripts — Cyrillic is constitutionally official
EXIT Festival in Novi Sad is one of Europe's largest music festivals, held in an 18th-century fortress

name: Slovakia
alphabet: Latin with diacritics: A, Á, Č, Š
facts: Has more castles per capita than any country in the world — over 100 castles and 425 chateaux
Bratislava is the only capital that borders two other countries — Austria and Hungary
The High Tatras' Gerlachovský štít is the highest peak at 2,655 m
Slovakia leads the world in cars produced per capita — Volkswagen, Kia, PSA, and Jaguar Land Rover all have plants here
The Slovak language and Czech are mutually intelligible — speakers can usually understand each other

name: Slovenia
alphabet: Latin alphabet plus Č, Š, Ž
facts: About 60% of Slovenia is covered in forest — among the most forested countries in Europe
Postojna Cave hosts the famed 'human fish' (olm), a blind salamander that can live a century
Lake Bled has a tiny island with a 17th-century church — a postcard image of the Alps
Slovenes are passionate about beekeeping — the Carniolan honey bee is the national emblem
Was the first former Yugoslav republic to join the eurozone (2007)

name: Spain
facts: Has 50 UNESCO World Heritage Sites
La Sagrada Família has been under construction since 1882
Spanish is the 2nd most spoken language by native speakers
Spain invented the mop, submarine, and stapler
La Tomatina: 40,000 kg of tomatoes hurled in the world's largest food fight

name: Sweden
facts: Invented the safety match, zipper, and Bluetooth
Has a 'right to roam' — you can legally camp anywhere in nature
ABBA, IKEA, Spotify, and Minecraft all come from Sweden
Sweden has more than 220,000 islands
Recycles 99% of all household waste

name: Switzerland
facts: Has not been at war since 1815
The World Wide Web was invented at CERN in Switzerland
Invented milk chocolate and the Swiss Army Knife
Has 4 official languages: German, French, Italian, Romansh
Bern means 'bears' — there are still real bears kept in the city

name: Turkey
facts: Istanbul is the only city in the world spanning two continents
Turkey introduced coffee to Europe via the Ottoman Empire
The first wheat and barley were planted here 10,000 years ago
Has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than all of South America
Santa Claus (St. Nicholas) was born in what is now Turkey

name: Ukraine
facts: Largest country entirely within Europe
Kyiv's Arsenalna metro station is the world's deepest at 105.5 m
The helicopter was invented by Ukrainian Igor Sikorsky in 1939
Ukraine is the 'breadbasket of Europe' — produces 25% of global grain
The Chernobyl Exclusion Zone is now a thriving wildlife sanctuary

name: United Kingdom
facts: Invented the World Wide Web, steam engine, and telephone
The British Empire once covered a quarter of the Earth's surface
London was the world's largest city from 1831 to 1925
Shakespeare invented over 1,700 words we still use today
UK has no written constitution — one of only 3 countries without one

name: Vatican City
facts: Smallest country in the world — about 0.49 km² and only ~800 residents
Has its own postal system, currency (the euro), and Latin ATMs ('Inserito scheda')
St. Peter's Basilica is the largest church in the world by interior
The Swiss Guard, founded in 1506, are the world's smallest army — and only protect the Pope
The Vatican Library holds an estimated 1.1 million books, including some of the rarest in existence

name: Antigua and Barbuda
facts: Antigua claims to have 365 beaches — 'one for every day of the year'
Nelson's Dockyard in English Harbour is the only continuously operational Georgian-era naval dockyard in the world
Independent from Britain since 1981 — still part of the Commonwealth
Capital is St. John's, on Antigua — but most people live on the more lush island of Antigua
Cricket is huge — Sir Vivian Richards, one of the greatest batsmen ever, hails from Antigua

name: Bahamas
facts: Spans about 700 islands and 2,400 cays — only around 30 are inhabited
Has some of the deepest 'blue holes' on Earth — Dean's Blue Hole drops 202 m
Bahamas was the first place in the New World that Columbus made landfall in 1492
The Exuma Cays are home to the famous 'swimming pigs' that paddle out to greet boats
Pink Sand Beach on Harbour Island gets its color from crushed Foraminifera shells

name: Barbados
facts: Most easterly Caribbean island, sometimes called 'Little England' for its long British history
Birthplace of rum — sugar plantations distilled the first rum here in the 1600s
Ranked among the most developed countries in the Caribbean by the UN HDI
Pop superstar Rihanna was born in Saint Michael, Barbados in 1988
Mount Gay Rum, founded in 1703, is widely considered the world's oldest commercial rum brand

name: Belize
facts: The Belize Barrier Reef is the second-largest coral reef system in the world after Australia's Great Barrier Reef
The Great Blue Hole — a 124 m-deep marine sinkhole — is one of the world's most famous dive sites
Only English-speaking country in Central America — formerly British Honduras
Less than 400,000 people, but one of the most ethnically diverse populations in the region
The ancient Maya site of Caracol is bigger than modern Belize City

name: Canada
facts: Longest coastline in the world at 202,080 km
Holds 20% of the world's freshwater supply
Has more lakes than the rest of the world combined
Hockey was invented in Canada in the 1800s
The CN Tower was the world's tallest structure for 34 years

name: Costa Rica
facts: Costa Rica abolished its army in 1948 — money was redirected into education and healthcare
Around 25% of the country is protected national parkland
Hosts about 6% of the world's biodiversity in just 0.03% of its surface
Aims to be one of the first carbon-neutral countries — runs on >98% renewable electricity
The phrase 'Pura Vida' ('pure life') is a national motto

name: Cuba
facts: Largest island in the Caribbean
Cuba has nearly 90% literacy and one of the highest doctor-to-patient ratios in the world
Old Havana, with its colonial mansions and 1950s American cars, is a UNESCO site
Cuba developed the first successful lung-cancer vaccine, CIMAvax-EGF
Salsa, mambo, and cha-cha-cha all originated in Cuba

name: Dominica
facts: Has the world's second-largest hot lake — Boiling Lake — heated by a fumarole below
Pirates of the Caribbean was filmed here — Dominica's lush rainforests played 'Isla de Muerta'
Home to the last remaining Kalinago (Carib) reservation in the Caribbean
Often called the 'Nature Island' for its 365 rivers and untouched cloud forests
Dominica is one of the few countries with a sisserou (imperial) parrot on its flag

name: Dominican Republic
facts: Pico Duarte is the highest peak in the Caribbean at 3,098 m
Shares the island of Hispaniola with Haiti — first Spanish colony in the Americas
Santo Domingo's Zona Colonial is the oldest European-built city in the New World, founded 1496
Birthplace of merengue and bachata music
Baseball is a national obsession — produces more MLB players than any country outside the US

name: El Salvador
facts: Smallest country in mainland Americas
First country in the world to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender (2021)
Volcán de Izalco was nicknamed the 'Lighthouse of the Pacific' for its century of constant eruptions
Pacific surf town El Tunco draws surfers worldwide — Surf City is a national branding push
El Salvador is the most densely populated country in mainland Latin America

name: Grenada
facts: Known as the 'Spice Isle' — produces about a third of the world's nutmeg
Has the world's first underwater sculpture park, off the west coast
Was invaded by the United States in 1983 — one of the smallest countries the US has ever invaded
Grenada's flag features a nutmeg in the upper-left corner
Independent from Britain since 1974 — Queen Elizabeth was head of state until 2022

name: Guatemala
facts: Tikal is one of the largest ancient Maya cities — temples up to 70 m tall stand above the rainforest
Lake Atitlán is sometimes called the most beautiful lake in the world — ringed by volcanoes
Antigua Guatemala is a UNESCO Spanish colonial city framed by three volcanoes
Quetzal — the brilliant green national bird — also names the currency
Has more than 30 volcanoes; some, like Pacaya and Fuego, are very active

name: Haiti
facts: First independent Black republic in the world — won independence from France in 1804
Citadelle Laferrière is one of the largest fortresses in the Americas — built atop a 900 m peak
Shares the island of Hispaniola with the Dominican Republic
Vodou — recognized as an official religion since 2003 — blends African religions with Catholicism
Toussaint Louverture led one of the only successful slave uprisings in human history

name: Honduras
facts: Copán's Maya ruins were once a major art capital — home to spectacular hieroglyphic stairways
Roatán's barrier reef is part of the second-longest coral reef in the world
Honduras was the original 'banana republic' — the term came from US fruit company politics here
Capital Tegucigalpa is one of the few capitals with no railway
Has more than 90 protected areas covering nearly a quarter of the country

name: Jamaica
facts: Birthplace of reggae — Bob Marley is the country's most famous export
Sprinter Usain Bolt, the fastest man in history, was born in Jamaica
The Blue Mountains produce some of the world's most expensive coffee
Has the third-most Olympic medals per capita despite a population of only ~3 million
Jamaica was the first British Caribbean colony to gain independence (1962)

name: Mexico
facts: Birthplace of chocolate, corn, and avocados
Mexico City sinks 20 cm per year — built on a drained lake
The eagle-and-snake flag image comes from an Aztec legend
Mexico has 35 UNESCO World Heritage Sites
The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was built on a lake island

name: Nicaragua
facts: Largest country in Central America by area
Lake Nicaragua is the only freshwater lake in the world with sharks (bull sharks adapted from saltwater)
León's Old Cathedral is the oldest cathedral in Central America
Sandboarding down active Cerro Negro volcano is a national tourist draw
Granada, founded 1524, is one of the oldest European-built cities in the Americas

name: Panama
facts: The Panama Canal — opened in 1914 — connects Atlantic and Pacific Oceans through 80 km of locks
The only place in the world where you can watch the sun rise over the Pacific and set over the Atlantic
Adopted the US dollar (called the 'balboa' locally) in 1904 — has no central bank
About 40% of the country is forested — Darién Gap blocks the only break in the Pan-American Highway
Panama hat actually originated in Ecuador, but was popularized via Panama Canal workers

name: Saint Kitts and Nevis
facts: Smallest country in the Americas by both land area and population
Two-island federation — joined since 1983 — capital Basseterre is on St. Kitts
Mount Liamuiga, an active volcano, dominates St. Kitts
Brimstone Hill Fortress is a UNESCO site — called the 'Gibraltar of the West Indies'
Sugar was the lifeblood of the islands until 2005, when the industry was officially closed

name: Saint Lucia
facts: The twin Pitons — volcanic plugs rising 770 m straight from the sea — are a UNESCO site
Has won two Nobel Prizes — to Sir Arthur Lewis (Economics) and Derek Walcott (Literature)
Has the highest number of Nobel laureates per capita of any independent country
Sulphur Springs is the world's only 'drive-in' volcano
Ownership shifted between the British and French 14 times — and St. Lucia retains a French Creole influence

name: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines
facts: An archipelago of 32 islands and cays — the Grenadines stretch south to Grenada
Pirates of the Caribbean was filmed extensively here — Wallilabou Bay played 'Port Royal'
La Soufrière, an active volcano on St. Vincent, erupted spectacularly in April 2021
Tobago Cays Marine Park is one of the world's most pristine reefs and a popular yacht destination
Capital Kingstown's botanical garden is the oldest in the Western Hemisphere — 1765

name: Trinidad and Tobago
facts: Birthplace of steelpan — the only major acoustic instrument invented in the 20th century
Carnival in Port of Spain is one of the world's largest, alongside Rio's
Pitch Lake on Trinidad is the world's largest natural deposit of asphalt — about 100 acres
Limbo dancing originated in Trinidad
About 9 species of hummingbirds — Trinidad is sometimes called 'the land of the hummingbird'

name: United States
facts: Has the world's largest economy
Americans eat the equivalent of 100 acres of pizza per day
Has won more Nobel Prizes than any other country
The Library of Congress is the world's largest library
50 stars represent the 50 states — originally there were only 13

name: Australia
facts: The only country that is also a continent
Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest living structure
Kangaroos outnumber humans 2 to 1
21 of the world's 25 most venomous snakes live here
The platypus is one of only five egg-laying mammals on Earth

name: Fiji
facts: An archipelago of more than 330 islands; about a third are inhabited
Fijians invented bilo — the kava-drinking ceremony of finely-pounded yaqona root
Hosts the third-most Pacific war shipwrecks for divers — popular for wreck and reef diving
Suva is the largest city in the South Pacific outside Australia and New Zealand
Sevens rugby is a national passion — Fiji won Olympic gold in 2016 and 2020

name: Kiribati
facts: Spans more than 3.5 million km² of ocean across 33 atolls — only 811 km² of land
Only country to lie in all four hemispheres (north, south, east, and west)
Crossed the International Date Line eastward in 1995 — keeping the country on a single day
Caroline Island in Kiribati was first to greet the year 2000
Most Kiribati islands sit barely 2 m above sea level — among the most threatened by sea-level rise

name: Marshall Islands
facts: An archipelago of 29 atolls and 5 isolated islands
Bikini and Enewetak atolls were used by the US for nuclear weapons testing — 67 tests from 1946–1958
Marshallese stick charts mapped ocean swells centuries before satellite navigation
Population of about 42,000 — among the smallest of any country
Has free association with the United States since 1986 — uses the US dollar

name: Micronesia
facts: Made up of 607 islands — federation of four states: Yap, Chuuk, Pohnpei, and Kosrae
Yap is famous for rai stones — giant stone discs used as money, some 4 m across
Nan Madol, a 1,000-year-old stone city built on a coral reef on Pohnpei, is a UNESCO site
Chuuk Lagoon is one of the world's top wreck-diving sites — sunk Japanese WWII fleet
Has free association with the US — Micronesians can live and work in the US visa-free

name: Nauru
facts: Third-smallest country in the world by area — only 21 km²
Once one of the wealthiest nations per capita on Earth thanks to phosphate mining — now nearly exhausted
Has no official capital — government offices are scattered around Yaren District
About 80% of the country has been strip-mined for phosphate, leaving a barren center
World's smallest island nation — Nauru is just one island

name: New Zealand
language: English, Māori, New Zealand Sign Language
facts: First country to give women the right to vote in 1893
Māori name Aotearoa means 'Land of the Long White Cloud'
Lord of the Rings was filmed entirely in New Zealand
Has more sheep per person than almost any other country
New Zealand has the world's highest proportion of endangered species

name: Palau
facts: First country in the world to ban most chemical sunscreens to protect coral reefs (2020)
All visitors must sign the 'Palau Pledge' on arrival — a stamp to protect the environment
Jellyfish Lake holds millions of harmless, stingless golden jellyfish
Was administered by the United States until 1994 — last UN trust territory to gain independence
Rock Islands Southern Lagoon is a UNESCO site of about 445 limestone islands

name: Papua New Guinea
facts: Around 850 distinct languages — about 12% of all languages spoken on Earth
Has some of the world's last truly undiscovered tribes in remote highland valleys
Mount Wilhelm reaches 4,509 m — one of the highest peaks in Oceania
Tribal 'sing-sings' bring together different cultures with elaborate face paint and feather headdresses
Birds of paradise — found largely in PNG — display some of nature's most extravagant courtship

name: Samoa
facts: Skipped 30 December 2011 to switch from east of the International Date Line to west — to align with Australia and NZ
Robert Louis Stevenson lived and is buried in Samoa, on Mount Vaea above Apia
Tatau (the original word for 'tattoo') is sacred — the male pe'a covers the body from waist to knees
First Pacific island nation to gain independence in the modern era (1962)
About 80% of land is held in customary ownership — passed down through extended families

name: Solomon Islands
facts: More than 990 islands — sixth-largest archipelago in the world
Major WWII battles — Guadalcanal, Iron Bottom Sound — were fought here from 1942
Skull Island still preserves ritual skull caves from headhunting traditions
Some inland Solomon Islanders have naturally blond hair — independent of European ancestry
Capital Honiara on Guadalcanal is named after a local phrase meaning 'place of the easterly wind'

name: Tonga
facts: Only Pacific nation never colonized — kept its monarchy through the colonial era
Hosts a vast undersea volcano — Hunga Tonga–Hunga Ha'apai's 2022 eruption was one of the largest of the 21st century
First country to see the new day — sometimes called 'where time begins'
Tongans hold fakaleiti as a recognized 'third gender'
Whales migrate close to shore each year — Vava'u is one of few places where you can swim with humpbacks

name: Tuvalu
facts: Fourth-smallest country in the world — only 26 km² of land
Earns significant revenue from its '.tv' internet domain — leased to broadcasters
World's least-visited country — fewer than 2,000 tourists a year on average
Highest point is just 4.6 m — Tuvalu is among the most threatened by rising seas
Population around 11,000 — about half live on the main island, Funafuti

name: Vanuatu
facts: Mt. Yasur on Tanna is one of the most accessible active volcanoes in the world — you can stand on the rim
Often ranked the 'happiest' country in the world by the Happy Planet Index
Pentecost Island's land-divers leap from 30 m towers tied only to vines — origin of bungee jumping
Has more than 100 distinct local languages despite a population of only ~320,000
Bislama, an English-based pidgin, is the lingua franca

name: Argentina
facts: Tango was born in Buenos Aires in the 1880s
Largest Spanish-speaking country in the world by area
Patagonia holds the third-largest freshwater reserve on Earth
First dinosaur fossil was found here in 1882
Has more psychoanalysts per capita than any other country

name: Bolivia
facts: Salar de Uyuni — the world's largest salt flat at over 10,000 km² — becomes a giant mirror in the rainy season
La Paz is the highest administrative capital on Earth, sitting at about 3,650 m
Bolivia has two capitals: Sucre (constitutional) and La Paz (de facto)
Lake Titicaca, on the Peruvian border, is the highest navigable lake in the world
About half of Bolivia's population is indigenous — the highest share in the Americas

name: Brazil
facts: Contains 60% of the Amazon rainforest
More primate species here than anywhere else on Earth
Only Portuguese-speaking country in the Americas
The Amazon discharges 20% of all freshwater entering the oceans
Carnival in Rio attracts over 2 million people daily

name: Chile
facts: World's longest country north-to-south — over 4,300 km — but averages just 177 km wide
The Atacama Desert is the driest non-polar place on Earth — some weather stations have never recorded rain
Easter Island (Rapa Nui) and its moai statues belong to Chile, 3,500 km off the coast
Has more active volcanoes than almost any country — about 90 in the Andean spine
World's leading copper producer — about a third of global production

name: Colombia
facts: Only South American country with both Pacific and Caribbean coasts
World's largest producer of emeralds
More bird species here than any other country on Earth
Gabriel García Márquez, inventor of magical realism, was Colombian
The name Colombia derives from Christopher Columbus

name: Ecuador
facts: The Galápagos Islands inspired Darwin's theory of evolution — they belong to Ecuador
Quito and Cuenca are two of the most extensive and best-preserved colonial centers in the Americas
Mount Chimborazo's summit is the farthest point on Earth from the planet's center, due to the equatorial bulge
First country to recognize the rights of nature in its constitution (2008)
Uses the US dollar as official currency since 2000

name: Guyana
facts: Only English-speaking country in South America
Kaieteur Falls is among the world's most powerful single-drop waterfalls — about 226 m, four times Niagara
Recently became one of the world's fastest-growing economies thanks to massive offshore oil discoveries
Around 80% of the country is covered by Amazonian rainforest
The Rupununi Savannah is one of South America's most pristine wildlife regions

name: Paraguay
facts: Has two official languages — Spanish and Guarani — and Guarani is widely spoken even in cities
Itaipú Dam (shared with Brazil) is one of the largest hydroelectric plants in the world
Doubly landlocked — South America's only landlocked country with no coast on any sea or ocean
Asunción is one of the oldest continuously inhabited Spanish settlements in South America
Paraguay's flag is the only national flag with different designs on each side

name: Peru
facts: Machu Picchu sits at 2,430 meters above sea level
Home to the Inca Empire, the largest in pre-Columbian America
The Amazon River begins in the Peruvian Andes
Peru has 90 microclimates — more than almost any country
Potatoes were first cultivated in Peru around 8,000 years ago

name: Suriname
facts: Smallest country in South America by area
Around 93% of Suriname is covered in forest — one of the highest percentages in the world
Dutch is the only official language — a legacy of Dutch colonial rule until 1975
Population is one of the most ethnically diverse in the world relative to its size
Central Suriname Nature Reserve — nearly 12% of the country — is a UNESCO site

name: Uruguay
facts: First country in the world to legalize cannabis nationally (2013)
Around 95% of the population lives in cities — one of the most urbanized in the Americas
Mate (yerba mate tea) is sipped from a shared gourd — a daily ritual
Won the very first FIFA World Cup in 1930 — and has won it twice
Almost all electricity comes from renewable sources — over 95% of the grid

name: Venezuela
facts: Angel Falls is the world's tallest uninterrupted waterfall at 979 m
Holds the world's largest proven oil reserves
Lake Maracaibo experiences 'Catatumbo lightning' — non-stop nightly storms of up to 280 strikes a minute
Mount Roraima — a flat-topped tepui shared with Brazil and Guyana — inspired Pixar's 'Up'
Caracas's Mount Ávila divides the city from the Caribbean — accessible by cable car

name: Kosovo
facts: Declared independence in 2008
Pristina is the largest city and capital
Uses the euro despite not being in the EU
The country is known for its mountain landscapes and monasteries
It is recognized by many countries, though not universally
